summary: Large naming-only cleanup that renamed many common fighter symbols from the generic `ft_` prefix to the more specific `ftCo_` prefix, updating C definitions, headers, Doxygen records, and assembly call/relocation references across 199 files. The PR had no body or review comments, so the intent is inferred from the title, naming label, and diff: make ftCommon/common-action symbols visibly belong to the ftCo namespace without changing behavior.
what_changed: Renamed common fighter symbols from `ft_800...` form to `ftCo_800...` form throughout source and assembly references.
Updated branch/call targets in assembly, for example `bl ft_800D1E80` became `bl ftCo_800D1E80`, `bl ft_800D14E4` became `bl ftCo_800D14E4`, and `bl ft_800C0FCC` became `bl ftCo_800C0FCC`.
Updated address relocation operands in assembly, for example `ft_800DB464@ha/@l` became `ftCo_800DB464@ha/@l` and `ft_800DE508@ha/@l` became `ftCo_800DE508@ha/@l`.

Updated C definitions and prototypes, with examples in the diff such as `void ft_8009B390(...)` to `void ftCo_8009B390(...)`, `void ft_8008DCE0(...)` to `void ftCo_8008DCE0(...)`, and `bool ft_80091A4C(...)` to `bool ftCo_80091A4C(...)`.
Propagated the namespace cleanup across many ftCommon action files, including Damage, Guard, ItemThrow, FallSpecial, AttackAir, Attack1, AttackS4, Pass, Lift, Wait, and related headers.
Updated external consumers outside ftCommon, including fighter initialization/special files, items, effects, game/stage assembly, and player code, so cross-system references remained consistent.
smart_moves: Performed the prefix change consistently across C, headers, Doxygen, and handwritten/remaining assembly, avoiding mixed symbol namespaces.
Kept address suffixes intact while only changing the namespace prefix, preserving the useful decomp convention of unknown functions retaining their address component.
Included assembly relocation labels as well as `bl` call targets, which is necessary for function-pointer/callback references such as `@ha` and `@l` operands.
Used `ftCo_` rather than a broader `ftCommon_` prefix for common-action symbols, matching the existing file/module naming style seen in paths like `ftCommon/ftCo_Damage.c` and `ftCo_Guard.c`.
The added and deleted line counts are nearly equal, supporting that this was a mechanical rename rather than a semantic gameplay or matching change.
decomp_lessons: When moving a family of symbols into a clearer namespace, update all consumers in one sweep: source definitions, public headers, dox stubs, assembly calls, and assembly relocation references.

For unknown decompiled functions, a refined subsystem prefix plus the original address, such as `ftCo_800D1E80`, is a useful intermediate name: it improves searchability without claiming a precise semantic name.
Assembly references are part of the symbol graph; a rename is incomplete if only C prototypes are changed.
Large mechanical renames should preserve signatures, addresses, and call structure so they do not interfere with matching work.

Subsystem prefixes should distinguish broad fighter code (`ft`), common fighter/common-action code (`ftCo`), and other existing namespaces such as `ftCommon`, `ftParts`, and character-specific prefixes.
naming_conventions: `ftCo_` is the preferred prefix for ftCommon/common fighter action symbols that previously used generic `ft_` address names.
Keep the original hex address suffix for unnamed or not-yet-semantically-named functions, e.g. `ft_800D4F24` to `ftCo_800D4F24`.
Character-specific files keep their own prefixes such as `ftKb_`, `ftCh_`, `ftKp_`, `ftSk_`, while calls into common fighter code use `ftCo_`.
Existing specific namespaces such as `ftCommon_`, `ftParts_`, `Fighter_`, and `ftAnim_` were not collapsed into `ftCo_` in the shown diff; this cleanup targeted the generic `ft_` common symbols.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Mechanical assembly symbol rename: update `bl` operands without altering instruction bytes/comments or control flow.
Update `lis`/`addi` relocation pairs using `symbol@ha` and `symbol@l` when they point at renamed callbacks or function addresses.
Preserve address comments and original instruction encodings, allowing the assembly to remain match-equivalent while improving symbol names.
Because this was a rename-only change, matching risk is low if every reference is updated consistently.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the PR slice.
No reviewer rationale or requested follow-up was available; conclusions are based on the title, naming label, changed-file set, and diff evidence.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that convert `ftCo_800...` address-only names into semantic names.
Search for remaining generic `ft_800` symbols under ftCommon to see whether the prefix cleanup was completed.
Check whether later header organization moved common-action prototypes out of `ft_0877.h` into more specific `ftCo_*` headers.
Audit assembly files for stale `ft_` references to common fighter functions after this rename.
